topic: The Mystery of Why Jupiter Is So Massive
title: How Jupiter Grew Into the Solar System's Giant

lesson.learn: Jupiter likely began with a solid core that formed very early in the young Solar System. Once that core became large enough, its gravity could pull in gas from the surrounding protoplanetary disk faster than the disk was being dispersed. That runaway growth is why Jupiter ended up with more mass than all the other planets combined and became the dominant planet in the Solar System.
lesson.simpler: Jupiter got huge because it formed early, built a big core, and then grabbed a lot of gas before the young Sun's disk disappeared.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Jupiter became massive because it formed fast enough to capture enormous amounts of hydrogen and helium before the protoplanetary disk was gone.

learnImageCredit.creator: ESO VLT/SPHERE - Monash University - Iain Hammond et al., adapted and mixed by Meli_thev

lesson.deeperDive: The leading model for Jupiter's birth is core accretion: a solid core forms first, then a gas giant grows rapidly once it crosses a critical mass threshold. Some research also explores disk instability as a faster alternative, but core accretion remains the standard explanation because it fits the Solar System's structure well. Jupiter's gravity likely influenced the asteroid belt, shepherded material in the early disk, and affected the migration history of the other giant planets. Even today, Jupiter helps shape the impact environment of the inner Solar System by both deflecting and redirecting small bodies.
lesson.funFact: Jupiter spins so fast that its day is just under 10 hours, even though the planet is enormous.
references: Review articles on core accretion and disk instability

people: Galileo Galilei - first observed Jupiter's major moons

years: Early Solar System - Jupiter begins forming
